data.title: Citi’s ‘SMID value creators’ stocks: GAP, CHWY, FHN, and more
data.description:
Citi Research analysts updated their “SMID value creators” list, removing Globant (GLOB).

The “SMID Value Creators List” names buy-rated stocks by Citi analysts with market caps between $1-15B.

Analysts select stocks from the universe with a bottom-up fundamental methodology and their equity strategy views. In addition, “we consider growth prospects relative to the valuation set up, health of the balance sheet, as well as where stocks fall within our SIGN preferences, thematic, and factor work,” said Scott Chronert, Citi Research head of US equity strategy, in a note.

Analysts also look for operating leverage that would impact profitability and prefer names that are top picks by the team “but maintain flexibility to align buy rated stocks with our market, sector, and thematic views.”

The following are 20 stocks, equally weighted. The group in total has generated 33.2% year-to-date, compared to 19.2% for the Russell 2500 (BATS:SMMD).

Here is the list:

  • GAP Inc. (GAP) – Price target: $32.00
  • RH (RH) – Price target: $355.00

data.title: Bipartisan group to push for break up of pharmacy-benefit managers (update)
data.description:
Update 12:40pm: Updates shares, adds link to proposed bill.

A bipartisan group of lawmakers introduced legislation to break up pharmacy-benefit managers.

Sen. Elizabeth Warren (D. Mass) and Josh Hawley (R. MO) proposed a bill that would force companies that own health insurers or pharmacy-benefits managers to sell off their pharmacy business, according to a statement from the lawmakers on Wednesday. A companion house bill was also expected to be introduced on Wednesday, according to a WSJ report, which broke the news of the proposed bill.

The three biggest PBMs include CVS Health's (NYSE:CVS) Caremark, Cigna's (NYSE:CI) Express Scripts and UnitedHealth's (NYSE:UNH) OptumRx. Shares of CVS, Cigna and UnitedHealth all slid 5%.

"PBMs have manipulated the market to enrich themselves -- hiking up drug costs, cheating employers, and driving small pharmacies out of business, " Warren said, according to the statement. "My new bipartisan bill will untangle these conflicts of interest by reining in these middlemen."

The bipartisan bills come after the Federal Trade Commission filed suit against Optum Rx, Caremark Express Scripts in late September, accusing the trio of inflating the prices of insulin drugs to boost profits.

The FTC in July released a report as part of an investigation into pharmacy benefit managers (PBMs), accusing them of profiting at the expense of patients and independent pharmacies, thus contributing to higher drug prices.

data.title: SA Charts: How did bitcoin miners perform in November?
Candel stock rockets on Phase 3 results for CAN-2409 (update)
data.description:
Candel Therapeutics (NASDAQ:CADL) stock rocketed as much as 217% Wednesday after the company reported positive Phase 3 results for its drug candidate CAN-2409 in the treatment of localized prostate cancer.

The stock recent sold for $6.64, up 44% from Tuesday's close, at around 12:30 p.m. ET.

The study, which met its primary endpoint, showed statistically significant improvement in disease-free survival in patients who received CAN-2409 plus valacyclovir combined with standard of care, compared to standard of care alone, according to a statement.

The clinical trial was conducted under a Special Protocol Assessment, meaning the data could be sufficient for the company to seek FDA approval for the drug for that indication.

Candel (CADL) also reported that a Phase 2 study of CAN-2409 as a monotherapy in patients with localized prostate cancer failed to show statistically significant numerical improvement in time to radical treatment and the percentage of negative biopsies at one-year post-treatment.

Seeking Alpha analyst Galzus Research said it believes the Phase 3 results support a potential FDA submission by mid-2025, but noted the company may have to conduct an equity raise due to high operational expenses and a limited cash runway.

“Viral therapies like CAN-2409 face commercialization challenges, but the strong Phase 3 data justifies maintaining a Buy rating for CADL stock,” Galzus added in its note.

(Updates story with latest stock moves, commentary from Galzus Research.)

data.title: Nasdaq punches above 20,000 points, S&P rises inflation data cements Fed rate-cut bets
data.description:
U.S. stocks were mostly higher Wednesday afternoon, as an in-line consumer inflation report solidified expectations of another quarter-point rate cut by the Federal Reserve next week.

The tech-concentrated Nasdaq Composite (COMP:IND) marked a milestone by crossing above 20,000 points for the first time ever, beefing up its +30% gain in 2024. The Nasdaq Composite and the S&P 500 (SP500) were each on course to halt a two-day slide, with the November inflation report driving up odds of a December rate-cut to 96% from 89% a day earlier, according to the CME FedWatch tool.

The Nasdaq (COMP:IND) +1.7% at 20,022.79 points. The benchmark S&P 500 (SP500) +0.9% while the Dow (DJI) gauge of blue-chip stocks hugged the flat line around ~44,245.

Six of the 11 sectors on the S&P 500 (SP500) rose, topped by the Communication Services +2.6%, Consumer Discretionary and Information Tech groups. All the Magnificent 7 tech behemoths climbed, with Tesla (TSLA) +3.2% and touching its highest price since 2021.

The consumer price index (CPI) for November from the U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics rose 0.3% M/M, the highest reading since April and matching the consensus estimate. Headline CPI climbed 2.7% Y/Y. Core CPI at +0.3% M/M was as expected, and it increased 3.3% on a Y/Y basis.

"The increase in the inflation rate (2.7% vs 2.6%) won’t be enough to spoil Christmas – the Fed is going to cut rates another 25 bps next week and that should enable markets to rally into year-end," Chris Zaccarelli, chief investment officer at Northlight Asset Management, said. The "series is a little noisy from month-to-month," he said, but "we believe the Fed is likely to look through these fluctuations and continue on their easing path."

Treasury yields initially fell after the CPI report but later turned mixed. The 10-year Treasury yield (US10Y) moved up 2 basis points to 4.25%, while the shorter-end 2-year yield (US2Y) was down 1 basis point at 4.15%.

The Federal Open Market Committee at its December 17-18 meeting is widely expected to reduce the fed funds rate to 4.25%-4.5%. November's CPI report indicates there is still work to do for policymakers in terms of getting inflation to move further toward their 2% target.

"Today's inflation data probably do not represent a sea change in the outlook for the Federal Reserve. Over the course of 2024, inflation has continued to slow, albeit more gradually than many had hoped it would at the start of the year," Wells Fargo's Sarah House said.

"We believe the FOMC will continue to reduce the federal funds rate next year in an effort to move monetary policy to a less restrictive position. We look for 100 bps of rate cuts from the FOMC over the next 12 months, with 25 bps rate cuts at next week's FOMC meeting and the March, June and September meetings next year," House added.

The equity market's bull run had stalled over the past two days ahead of the November CPI data. Still, Wall Street is on track to end another year with hefty gains.

Wednesday's spotlight was also grabbed by a few key earnings reports. Macy's (M) -5.5% after the department store chain delivered a quarterly decline in comparable sales and cut its annual adjusted profit per share guidance.

Heavily shorted GameStop (GME) +11%, after the video game retailer delivered a surprise quarterly profit.

Adobe (ADBE) stock will be in focus ahead of the creative software maker's results after the closing bell.

data.title: Reaction Roundup: Experts weigh in on November consumer inflation report
data.description:
Wall Street's focus on Wednesday was on the November U.S. consumer inflation report, in which both the headline number and the core figure came in-line with expectations.

According to the U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics, headline consumer price index (CPI) accelerated in November to +0.3% M/M, the highest reading since April. Meanwhile, core CPI held steady at +0.3% M/M for a fourth straight month.

On a Y/Y basis, headline CPI increased 2.7% in November, inching up from October's +2.6%. Core CPI climbed 3.3%, matching October.

U.S. stocks (SP500) opened higher after the data, as the benign inflation report cemented expectations of a quarter point interest rate cut by the Federal Reserve next week. Here are some exchange-traded funds that track the benchmark index: (SPY), (VOO), (IVV), (RSP), (SSO), (UPRO), (SH), (SDS), and (SPXU).

See below for various reactions to the CPI report:

Mohamed El-Erian, chief economic advisor at Allianz:

"The U.S. inflation numbers were in line with the consensus forecasts. This is relatively good news in a situation where the balance of risk was perceived by some to favor somewhat hotter numbers. Less good is that this is the fourth straight month of 0.3% core inflation prints.

The longer this continues, the more it will confirm something that I have discussed several times in the past - namely, that the Federal Reserve will have to choose between (i) accepting to operate for a while with a relatively stable inflation rate that is above its 2% target or (ii) redouble its efforts to meet the 2% target and risk pulling the rug from under U.S. economic exceptionalism."

Jeremy Schwartz, global chief investment officer at WisdomTree:

"Inflation news again even better than official reports! Monthly shelter improves but still almost 5% last 12M. Real world shelter indicators are much lower, only 1.2%. Subbing in this real-time shelter, CPI improves from 2.7% to just 1.5%! Core Inflation subbing real time shelter: 1.8% (not 3.3% official)

Bottom line: inflation is right on target! The Fed should keep cutting until (it) gets to a neutral rate around 3.5-4%."

E.J. Antoni, research fellow at The Heritage Foundation:

"Oops - back over 0.3% M/M.

Average hourly wages are up $5.68 (19.0%) since Jan '21 but after adjusting for inflation, that's a drop of $0.39 (1.3%) - the total difference of $6.07/hr can be thought of as the hourly inflation tax accrued under the Biden admin; no wonder people are mad about the economy.

Food price inflation is back on the rise as the monthly increase in prices doubled from Oct (0.2%) to Nov (0.4%) - the situation isn't getting better, but deteriorating. We're not trending to 2.0% inflation, and we never were, certainly not since Jan '21."

Jason Furman, former deputy director of the U.S. National Economic Council:

"The last mile is proving very, very stubborn.

My judgment about underlying inflation shifted from 2.5% to 3.0% to ~2.5%. I'm still optimistically clinging to ~2.5%, enough noisy items on the upside to discount this report a little bit. But still, looking like last mile is harder."

Samuel Tombs, chief U.S. economist at Pantheon Macroeconomics:

"We estimate that today's U.S. CPI data are consistent with a 0.20% increase in the core PCE deflator in November. Tomorrow's PPI data might yet move the dial, but for now, it looks like the FOMC can ease again next week with a clear conscience."

Nick Timiraos, the Wall Street Journal's Fed watcher:

"Falling prices (deflation) of core goods over the last 18 months helped deliver a decent chunk of the disinflation we've seen.

That has now ended, with higher car prices pushing up core goods 0.3% on the month (after +0.05% in October and +0.17% in September)."

Ernie Tedeschi, director of economics at The Budget Lab at Yale:

"Just a few quick points.

1. Both PCE & CPI 12-month inflation do appear to be flattening out in recent months.

2. The Fed's target is in terms of PCE. Core PCE YY in Oct was 2.8%, v core CPI of 3.3%.

3. Market-based core PCE, an even better trend measure, is 2.4%.

All of which is to say, inflation is not at target, but it's much closer to target than CPI suggests."

More on the markets

  • November CPI: General Disinflation Stalls But Services And Shelter Cool
  • November CPI: Second Consecutive Inflation Increase, Not Cause For Concern
  • S&P 500: A Year-End Record, A Challenging 2025, And A 'Lost Decade'
  • Safe Haven Demand Will Keep Gold Strong In 2025
  • Oil Market Set To Be In Surplus Despite OPEC+ Action

data.title: 10 BofA U.S. options overwriting ideas
data.description:
BofA Securities analysts selected 10 overwriting candidates with at least 5% premium and 6% potential upside.

“Overwriting outperforms a long-only position if the underlying stock declines, but if the stock rises, it can underperform if the call strike is not sufficiently high,” wrote Arjun Goyal, equity-linked analyst at BofA.

He said that investors who may be concerned about missing out on the upside when overwriting could consider these candidates within the Russell 1000 (NYSEARCA:IWB) that may see at least 6% gain by Jan. 17, earn a minimum premium of 5%, and have underlying notional option volume of at least $5M.

Here are the candidates. All have a Jan. 17 expiration:

  • Avis (CAR) – Strike: 105; Call and dividend premium: 5.4%; Call away return: 10.2%
  • AppLovin (APP) – Strike: 350; Call and dividend premium: 7.7%; Call away return: 9.9%
  • Micron Technology (MU) – Strike: 105; Call and dividend premium: 6.4%; Call away return: 8.5%
  • Dexcom (DXCM) – Strike: 80; Call and dividend premium: 5.6%; Call away return: 7.6%
  • Carnival (CCL) – Strike: 26; Call and dividend premium: 5.1%; Call away return: 7.5%
  • United Airlines (UAL) – Strike: 97.5; Call and dividend premium: 6%; Call away return: 7.5%
  • Coherent (CHR) – Strike: 105; Call and dividend premium: 5.9%; Call away return: 6.9%
  • ARM (ARM) – Strike: 140; Call and dividend premium: 6.5%; Call away return: 6.8%
  • Exact Sciences (EXAS) – Strike: 62.5; Call and dividend premium: 5.7%; Call away return: 6.8%
  • Albemarle (ALB) – Strike: 110; Call and dividend premium: 6.4%; Call away return: 6.7%

data.title: REITs stand to benefit from increased commercial real estate transactions in 2025
data.description:
REITs stand to benefit from increased commercial real estate transactions next year, and make inroads into institutional real estate portfolios, the 2025 REIT Outlook by Nareit showed.

There is a possibility for an environment with both moderating interest rates and robust economic growth, the trade association said in a report.

However, risks to REITs include soft property fundamentals in some sectors, higher interest rates reflecting fiscal imbalances, and shifting tariff policies that restrain commercial real estate performance.

For 2025, REITs are expected to see accretive growth opportunities as commercial real estate transactions increase.

An economic soft landing, public-private real estate valuations returning to equilibrium, and the resulting revival in property transactions market are expected to boost the commercial real estate market in the coming year.

A revival in the currently stalled commercial real estate transaction market is likely as public and private real estate values become more in sync, the report noted.

Specialization, scale, innovation, and sustainability are the megatrends that will define real estate for the next decade, according to Nareit.

REITs are expected to continue to collaborate with institutional investors, including pensions and sovereign wealth funds, seeking access to commercial real estate as investors increasingly use REITs to access high-quality properties.

data.title: Q32 stock craters 71% on mixed Phase 2 data for bempikibart (update)
data.description:
• Shares of Q32 Bio (NASDAQ:QTTB) plunged 71% Wednesday after the company reported mixed Phase 2 results for its drug bempikibart in the treatment of atopic dermatitis, or AD, and alopecia areata, or AA.
  • Q32 Bio (QTTB) said that while a Phase 2a study showed promising clinical activity for the drug in patients AA, the part B portion of a Phase 2a study for AD failed to meet its primary endpoint, according to a statement.
  • Based on the results, the company has decided to proceed with developing the drug for AA but not AD. It plans to enroll around 20 additional patients in a Part B expansion of the Phase 2a study for AA, which will include a loading regimen.
  • The company said it has published an updated investor presentation with details of the drug update on its website.
  • Raymond James downgraded the stock to outperform from strong buy following the news, saying it "misjudged market reaction" to the "all-too-plausible mixed results."
  • The investment firm also cut its price target for the stock to $22 from $90.
  • (Story updated with recent stock move, Raymond James downgrade.)

data.title: Wells Fargo CEO speaks about OCC lifting, Basel III, consumer spending trends, and more
data.description:
The termination of the OCC consent order issued in 2016 for sales practices misconduct at Wells Fargo (NYSE:WFC) puts them in a position of growth, said its CEO.

Wells Fargo CEO Charles W. Scharf spoke about consumer and corporate spending trends, the election implications, deposit behavior, capital markets opportunities, and more at this year’s Goldman Sachs Financial Services Conference.

He said that spending trends into the year-end has seen continued strength for both consumers and corporates.

“Every business that we have – with the exception of the mortgage business – is one that we feel have great growth prospects today.”

Scharf also said that, although he expects economic volatility, “we do well when the U.S. does well.”

Net interest income and deposit behavior

Scharf said that consumers are certainly less rate sensitive now.

“There is more deposit stability across both the commercial side and the consumer side,” he said. “We've actually had some growth in deposits, which allow us to bring down our non-customer related deposits, as well as the entire balance sheet.”

However, he said he has not seen an actual pickup in load demand.

“[We have not seen] inventories being built up because of potential tariffs…we've not seen that in any meaningful way [so far].”

Capital markets opportunities and the OCC consent order lifting

“In an environment where there's stronger loan growth – where you’re able to compete more on deposits – not having the asset cap will be a huge advantage for us that we just don't have today and haven't had over the last six or seven years.”

Wells Fargo confirmed in February that the Office of the Comptroller of the Currency lifted a sales practices misconduct consent order, issued in 2016, which required the bank to overhaul how it offered and sold products and services to consumers, and to take additional protective measures for customers and employees.

The consent order, “forced us to undo a lot of the things related to how the consumer and small business bank was run,” he said. “We eliminated compensation plans. We eliminated branch profit-and-loss. We eliminated reporting that would potentially incent people to sell things, because there was this big question mark around, where did we have the right controls in place?”

The OCC lifting, he added, was incredibly important for Wells Fargo.

“[It was an] incredibly important statement about how objectively they're looking at us, and our ability to complete the work, and how they're going to judge us,” he said. “But it also says that we do have the controls in place, and we can now start adding things back in a reasonably controlled way, which will put us in a position to grow differently than we've been able to grow.”

Scharf spoke about the implementation of a series of new account opening procedures “that are based digitally inside the company.”

He said he is “very bullish about the opportunities.”

Basel III

Scharf also spoke about Basel III, which is a set of international regulations to establish requirements for banks’ capital adequacy, liquidity and stress testing. It was created in response to the great financial crisis of 2007-2008 to help banks deal with economic downturns and remain liquid. The third phase of implementation is expected to take effect mid-2025.

“We absolutely want it to be finalized,” he said. “It's just a strange position to be in [having] some of the most significant companies in this country unsure of what their capital requirements are going to be.”

“It's a crazy way to run systems, so we absolutely would love to see closure in terms of what this all means…in a thoughtful way that makes sense.”
